era_context: The early 770s CE are characterized by Charlemagne's expansion in Western Europe, the consolidation of the Abbasid Caliphate under al-Mansur, the Tang Dynasty's recovery from the An Lushan Rebellion, and the Byzantine iconoclastic controversy under Constantine V. This is an era of significant military campaigns, administrative reorganization, and religious conflict.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records; archaeological dating imprecise)

events.title: Charlemagne destroys the Irminsul (Saxon holy site)
events.description: Charlemagne launches a military campaign against the Saxon peoples and destroys the Irminsul, a sacred Saxon pagan shrine. This act is both a military conquest and a religious statement, symbolizing the Frankish determination to conquer and Christianize the Saxon territories. The destruction of the Irminsul marks the beginning of the prolonged Saxon Wars (772–804 CE), one of Charlemagne's most significant military campaigns. The event is attested in the Royal Frankish Annals and represents a turning point in the Christianization of northern Europe.
key_figures: Saxon leaders (names not preserved in contemporary sources)
events.certainty_note: The destruction of the Irminsul in 772 is well-documented in the Royal Frankish Annals, a near-contemporary source. The date is certain.

events.title: Abbasid Caliphate: Suppression of Kharijite revolts
events.description: Caliph al-Mansur (r. 754–775 CE) continues military operations against Kharijite rebels, who reject Abbasid authority and represent a significant internal threat to the caliphate. The suppression of Kharijite revolts is a major focus of al-Mansur's reign, and the year 772 falls within this period of internal consolidation. These campaigns are part of the broader effort to establish Abbasid control over the Islamic world.

sources.name: al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Al-Mansur's campaigns against Kharijites are well-attested, but specific events in 772 are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Tang Dynasty: Military operations in Central Asia and the north
events.region: Tang Dynasty / Central Asia / Northern Frontier
events.description: The Tang Dynasty continues military operations to maintain control over Central Asian trade routes and defend against threats from the Tibetan Empire, the Uighur Khaganate, and other rivals. The year 772 falls within a period of sustained Tang military activity on multiple frontiers. These campaigns are essential to Tang efforts to project power westward and secure the Silk Road.
key_figures: Tang military commanders (names often not preserved)
events.certainty_note: Tang military activity in this period is well-attested, but specific campaigns in 772 are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Byzantine Empire: Iconoclasm under Constantine V
events.description: Emperor Constantine V (r. 741–775 CE) continues his enforcement of iconoclasm in the Byzantine Empire. The year 772 falls within the height of Constantine V's iconoclastic campaign, which had begun in 741. This policy remains deeply controversial and divisive within Byzantine society and the church. Constantine V's iconoclasm is one of the most significant religious controversies of the medieval Christian world.
events.certainty_note: Constantine V's reign and iconoclastic policies are well-documented. The year 772 falls securely within his reign.

events.title: Tibetan Empire: Expansion and consolidation
events.description: The Tibetan Empire, under the Yarlung Dynasty, continues its expansion and consolidation of power on the Tibetan Plateau and in surrounding regions. The year 772 falls within a period of Tibetan military expansion and increasing competition with the Tang Dynasty for control of Central Asian territories. The Tibetan Empire would become one of the major powers of Central Asia and a significant rival to Tang China.
key_figures: Tibetan emperors (names not consistently preserved in contemporary sources)
events.certainty_note: The general expansion of the Tibetan Empire in the 770s is attested, but specific events in 772 are not clearly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The destruction of the Irminsul is well-documented, but the religious significance attributed to it in some historiography may be overstated. The Irminsul was a sacred site to the Saxons, but the extent to which its destruction unified Saxon resistance or was primarily a military/political act is debated. Modern scholarship emphasizes that the Saxon Wars were not primarily religious conflicts but rather struggles for territorial control and political dominance. The dating of specific Abbasid, Tang, and Tibetan military operations to 772 is often uncertain; sources provide general patterns rather than precise annual records.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 772 CE is strong for Western Europe (Frankish sources), moderate for the Byzantine Empire and Tang China, and sparse for Central Asia, Tibet, and other regions. The destruction of the Irminsul is one of the best-documented events of the era. However, sources for Scandinavia, Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania are absent. The surviving sources reflect the interests of literate elites and likely underrepresent economic, social, and cultural history. The dating of some events shows uncertainty across sources, particularly for Central Asian and Tibetan events.

graph_edges.note: The 772 destruction of the Irminsul initiates the Saxon Wars, which intensify in 775
The 772 campaign is the beginning of the Saxon Wars (772–804)
Al-Mansur's 772 campaigns against Kharijites are part of his broader consolidation of the Abbasid state
